topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot rattled, a sound that usually signaled routine delivery, but today, it was something else. Tucked amongst the junk flyers and bills was a thick, cream-colored card, entirely blank save for a single, elegant script at the bottom: "Happy Birthday." My fingers, usually steady when sorting through the clutter, froze mid-air. I picked it up, the paper surprisingly heavy, and turned it over. No address, no signature, just the hopeful, stark message. A cold knot tightened in my stomach. Who had this? And why had they left it here, addressed to me, without any context? I smoothed the card flat on the kitchen counter, my breath catching shallowly in my chest. Every instinct screamed at me to throw it away, to scrub the evidence from my doorstep, but I couldn't bring myself to discard it. I just stared at the plain white surface, waiting for something to happen.

[story 2]

I was standing by the front door, waiting for the postman, when I saw it. A card. Unaddressed, unsealed, resting squarely in the center of my mailbox. It was thick cardstock, the kind you usually see for formal invitations, and on it, in looping, unfamiliar handwriting, were the words: "Wishing you a wonderful day." My hand trembled as I reached for it. The texture felt strange under my fingertips, almost too smooth. As I pulled it out, the sound of the neighborhood traffic seemed to fade away. My gaze darted around the quiet street, suddenly hyper-aware of every shadow stretching across the lawn. I reread the message twice, the sentiment feeling heavy, almost accusatory. I slipped the card into my pocket, the fabric brushing against my skin, and hurried inside, my movements jerky, unable to settle into the ordinary rhythm of my afternoon.

[story 3]

It was late afternoon, and I was sorting through the accumulated mail, a task I usually found soothingly monotonous. Then I saw it—a card, stark white against the faded envelopes. It wasn't addressed; it was simply a birthday wish, written in a precise, almost calligraphic hand. A strange stillness descended upon the room. My heart began to beat a rapid, uneven rhythm against my ribs. I picked it up, turning it over and over, searching for any hidden detail, any clue that might explain its presence. There was nothing. Just the message, utterly impersonal yet intensely personal. A prickling sensation ran down my arms. I dropped the card onto the nearest surface with a soft thud, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge of the table. I couldn't look away from the blank space where a sender's name should have been.
